ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for kunafa; mapped to the closest defensible parent class 'pastry food product' (FOODON_03315091). A more specific term for shredded-wheat cheese pastry is not yet present in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry and cakes — sweet pastry with filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (sweet pastry with filling) is the closest defensible match for kunafa; no dedicated kunafa or kadaif code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for kunafa or kadaif pastry identified in the SNOMED CT International Edition as of 2024.

main_ingredients: rose water or orange blossom water

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dessert portion of kunafa is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a single-serve slice as commonly sold at Middle Eastern dessert stalls and Turkish restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheese pastry, baklava-type, with nuts and syrup (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for kadaif/knafeh-type cheese pastry composite
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for kunafa. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sweet cheese-filled pastry with syrup (closest analogous composite: shredded wheat pastry with cheese and sugar syrup). The input record values (energy 325 kcal, protein 9 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 32.5 g, sugar 22.5 g, sodium 175 mg, dietary fibre 1.0 g, saturated fat 10.0 g, cholesterol 40 mg, calcium 125 mg, iron 0.75 mg, trans fat 0.25 g) are consistent with the USDA analogous range for this food type and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrient detail not reported in the analogous USDA entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on sweet pastry with refined flour and sugar syrup (GI ~65, medium category)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for kunafa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: sweet pastry made with refined wheat flour and sugar syrup (baklava-type, GI ~65, medium category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 65.0 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 42.3. Carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 32.5 × 2 = 65.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (22.5 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat at 10.0 g/100 g contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit kunafa due to its high sugar load (22.5 g/100 g) and saturated fat (10.0 g/100 g), both of which promote vascular inflammation and insulin-driven sodium retention even at the relatively moderate sodium level of 175 mg/100 g; if consumed, request minimal syrup and restrict to a small portion (≤50 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 22.5 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate 32.5 g/100 g with only 1.0 g/100 g dietary fibre and estimated GI of 65 produces a very high glycaemic load of 42.3 per standard 200 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kunafa is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high sugar content (22.5 g/100 g), high glycaemic load (42.3 per 200 g serving), and negligible dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g); the sugar syrup soaking and refined kadaif pastry create rapid postprandial glucose excursions and should be avoided or replaced with a reduced-syrup, smaller-portion alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 10.0 g/100 g from butter and cheese substantially exceeds the recommended limit; trans fat 0.25 g/100 g and cholesterol 40 mg/100 g add further atherogenic burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kunafa poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its saturated fat content of 10.0 g/100 g (50% of the AHA daily limit in a single 200 g serving), the presence of trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and cholesterol (40 mg/100 g); individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and, if eaten occasionally, limit to a very small portion (≤50 g) without added syrup.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 10.0 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA pro-inflammatory threshold; refined sugar at 22.5 g/100 g promotes systemic inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kunafa is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because its saturated fat (10.0 g/100 g from butter and cheese) and high refined sugar (22.5 g/100 g from sugar syrup) both upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; avoidance is recommended, and pistachio garnish alone is insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kunafa (also spelled Knafeh, Kanafeh, or Künefe in Turkish) is a traditional Middle Eastern and Turkish dessert made from shredded phyllo pastry (kadaif) layered with a soft, unsalted white cheese — typically akkawi or fresh mozzarella — baked in a round tray until the pastry is golden and crisp, then immediately soaked in a fragrant sugar syrup flavoured with rose water or orange blossom water, and garnished with crushed pistachios. The result is a dessert with a crisp, golden exterior, a warm, stretchy, mildly salty cheese interior, and an intensely sweet syrup coating that balances the savoury cheese. Per 100 g, kunafa provides approximately 325 kcal, 9 g protein, 17.5 g fat (of which 10.0 g is saturated fat), 32.5 g carbohydrate, 22.5 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, 175 mg sodium, 125 mg calcium, and 40 mg cholesterol. Its estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium category), but the glycaemic load per standard 200 g serving is very high at approximately 42.3, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. Kunafa is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat, high sugar, and high glycaemic load. It is halal and vegetarian but contains gluten, dairy, and tree nuts (pistachios). In Singapore, kunafa is served at Turkish and Middle Eastern dessert stalls and is popular as a dessert or sweet snack. It is best consumed as an occasional treat in small portions.

What is the difference between kunafa and baklava?
Can I eat kunafa if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact kunafa entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from the closest USDA FoodData Central analogous composite (sweet cheese-filled shredded wheat pastry with sugar syrup) and are consistent with the input record values. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for kunafa is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the analogous USDA FoodData Central entry used for this adaptation. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: kunafa contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central analogous entry. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g/100 g is retained from the input record as consistent with butter-based pastry products in USDA data. The input source listed (HPB Singapore Foodo at foodo.sg) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it does not contain a kunafa entry; USDA FoodData Central was used instead as the approved secondary global source. Cultural tags 'Kebab' and 'Shawarma' from the input record have been removed as they are not relevant to kunafa. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore' which is the serving location, not the origin; region_of_origin has been corrected to 'Middle East (Turkey, Levant)'. Multilingual names for Chinese, Tamil, and Hindi are transliterations as no established loanword script form exists in standard dictionaries; these should be verified by a native-language reviewer. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for kunafa is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024.
